questions.prompt: What does a reasoning model do differently from a regular GPT-5-class model?

choices.text: It uses a smaller context window.

It generates hidden 'reasoning tokens' (a private chain of thought) before producing the visible output. You pay for both reasoning and output tokens. The result: much better performance on hard multi-step problems, at the cost of higher latency and bill per call.
It calls tools automatically without prompting.

questions.explanation: Reasoning models are trained to 'think' before answering. The thinking is structured, efficient, and invisible to your application — but it's billed as reasoning tokens at the output rate. The benefit is significantly higher quality on math, code, and multi-step planning. The cost is latency (the thinking takes time) and dollars (the tokens add up).

questions.prompt: Your chat product currently uses GPT-5-mini. Most turns are fine, but ~10% of customer questions involve multi-step planning that the bot gets wrong. What's the right pattern?

choices.text: Switch every call to a reasoning model at `high` effort.

Add a small classifier that detects 'hard' queries and routes them to a reasoning model. Keep simple queries on GPT-5-mini. You get quality on the cases that need it without paying reasoning costs on the 90% of easy turns.
Tell users to ask simpler questions.

questions.explanation: Selective routing is the standard production pattern for reasoning models. A tiny classification step (on a small/fast model) decides which route to take. Most traffic stays on the cheap fast path; only hard cases pay reasoning costs. Typical savings: 60–80% vs. always-on reasoning, with no quality loss on the hard cases.
